home.title: Biology — Revision
home.intro: Six core topics for G9 IB MYP Biology, with RU/EN glossary and links to the school's external resources.
home.externalNotice: External pages will open in a new tab if iframe is blocked.

punnett.title: Punnett Square
punnett.description: Enter two parent genotypes (e.g., Bb × Bb) — see all 4 offspring combinations with genotype + phenotype ratios.

cellDiagram.title: Cell Diagram — animal vs plant
cellDiagram.description: Hover any organelle to see its function. Toggle between animal and plant cells to compare.

heartDiagram.title: Heart Diagram
heartDiagram.description: Hover chambers and vessels — see how deoxygenated and oxygenated blood flow through the double circulation.

extras.title: Biology — Extras
extras.intro: Topics that extend beyond the G9 syllabus — depth on photosynthesis, hormones, mutations, biotech.

photoDepth.title: Photosynthesis — deeper
photoDepth.body: Two stages: light-dependent (in thylakoid membranes) — chlorophyll absorbs photons, water is split (H₂O → 2H⁺ + ½O₂ + 2e⁻), ATP and NADPH are made. Light-independent / Calvin cycle (in stroma) — CO₂ is fixed using ATP + NADPH to form glucose. Limiting factors: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature.

hormones.title: Hormones in depth
items: Insulin (pancreas, β-cells) — lowers blood glucose by promoting uptake into cells. Diabetes type 1: no insulin; type 2: cells resist insulin.
Glucagon (pancreas, α-cells) — raises blood glucose by releasing it from liver glycogen.
Adrenaline (adrenal medulla) — fight-or-flight: heart rate up, pupils dilate, glucose released.
Oestrogen / testosterone — secondary sexual characteristics during puberty.

genetics.title: Genetics — mutations and codons
genetics.body: DNA is read in triplets called codons; each codes for an amino acid (e.g., AUG = methionine = start). 64 possible codons but only 20 amino acids — code is redundant. Mutations: substitution (one base), insertion (gain), deletion (loss). Insertion/deletion shift the reading frame → potentially catastrophic. Sickle cell anaemia is caused by a single substitution in haemoglobin.

biotech.title: Biotechnology
items: Selective breeding — choose parents with desired traits over many generations (modern crops, livestock).
Genetic engineering — transfer genes between species using restriction enzymes + plasmids (e.g., insulin from bacteria).
Cloning — Dolly the sheep (1996) via nuclear transfer; plant tissue culture is routine.
CRISPR — precise gene editing tool (2012); revolutionary for medicine and agriculture.
Ethics — consent, biodiversity, unforeseen consequences are key MYP Criterion D considerations.

homeostasis.title: Homeostasis
homeostasis.body: Maintaining a constant internal environment despite external changes. Body temperature ~37 °C (vasodilation, sweating to cool; vasoconstriction, shivering to warm). Blood glucose ~5 mmol/L (insulin/glucagon balance). Water balance (kidneys, ADH). Negative feedback loops are the core mechanism.

cells.title: Cell Biology
cells.intro: Cells are the basic unit of life. Compare animal vs plant cells, understand specialized structures, and link cell function to organisms.

nucleus.name: Nucleus
nucleus.rule: Contains the DNA — the cell's genetic blueprint. Controls all cell activities by deciding which proteins are made. Surrounded by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) with pores for RNA to leave.
nucleus.tip: Most cells have ONE nucleus. Exceptions: red blood cells (none, to make room for haemoglobin) and skeletal muscle cells (many, to coordinate large fibres).

cytoplasm.name: Cytoplasm
cytoplasm.rule: Jelly-like fluid (mostly water + dissolved salts and proteins) filling the cell. Site of most chemical reactions in the cell, including glycolysis (first stage of respiration).
cytoplasm.tip: Don't confuse cytoplasm (the fluid) with cytosol (the fluid PART without organelles). At G9, cytoplasm is fine.

membrane.name: Cell membrane (plasma membrane)
membrane.rule: Phospholipid bilayer with proteins. Partially permeable — small/non-polar molecules pass freely, large/polar molecules need transport proteins. Controls what enters and leaves.
examples: O₂ and CO₂ pass freely (small, non-polar).
Glucose needs a transporter protein.
Ions (Na⁺, K⁺) need protein channels.

mitochondria.name: Mitochondria
mitochondria.rule: Site of aerobic respiration. Glucose + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ATP (energy). The inner membrane is folded (cristae) to maximise surface area for ATP-making enzymes.
mitochondria.tip: Cells that need LOTS of energy have MANY mitochondria: muscle cells, liver cells, sperm tails.

ribosome.name: Ribosomes
ribosome.rule: Tiny dots (not membrane-bound). Site of PROTEIN SYNTHESIS — they read mRNA and link amino acids together into a protein chain.
ribosome.tip: Found free in cytoplasm OR attached to rough endoplasmic reticulum. The MORE proteins a cell needs to make, the MORE ribosomes it has.

wall.name: Cell wall (plants, fungi, bacteria)
wall.rule: Rigid outer layer OUTSIDE the cell membrane. In plants made of CELLULOSE; in fungi chitin; in bacteria peptidoglycan. Gives the cell SHAPE and supports the plant.
wall.watchOut: Animal cells do NOT have a cell wall — that's why they have flexible, irregular shapes.

chloroplast.name: Chloroplast (plants only)
chloroplast.rule: Contains green pigment chlorophyll. Site of PHOTOSYNTHESIS: CO₂ + H₂O + light → glucose + O₂. Has internal stacks (thylakoids) where light is absorbed.
chloroplast.tip: Found mostly in leaf cells, especially palisade mesophyll — packed near the upper surface to catch light.

vacuole.name: Permanent vacuole (plants only)
vacuole.rule: Large fluid-filled sac in the centre of plant cells. Stores 'cell sap' (water, salts, sugars). When full, it presses outwards giving the plant TURGOR — what keeps stems upright.
vacuole.tip: When a plant wilts, vacuoles have lost water → cells become flaccid. Animal cells have only tiny temporary vacuoles.

animalVsPlant.title: Animal vs plant cells
animalVsPlant.shared: Both have: nucleus, cytoplasm, cell membrane, mitochondria, ribosomes
animalVsPlant.plantOnly: Plant only: cell wall, chloroplasts, permanent vacuole, often regular shape

redBlood.name: Red blood cell (erythrocyte)
redBlood.rule: Carries oxygen from lungs to body tissues via haemoglobin (Hb + O₂ ⇌ HbO₂).
examples: BICONCAVE shape: increases surface area for O₂ absorption.
NO NUCLEUS: more room for haemoglobin (~270 million per cell).
FLEXIBLE: can squeeze through narrow capillaries.
redBlood.tip: Lifespan ~120 days. Broken down in spleen and liver, recycled.

neurone.name: Neurone (nerve cell)
neurone.rule: Carries electrical impulses around the nervous system.
examples: LONG AXON: can be over 1 metre (from spinal cord to foot).
DENDRITES: branched ends receive signals from many other neurones.
MYELIN SHEATH: insulates the axon → impulses travel up to 100 m/s.
neurone.tip: Three types: sensory (in → CNS), motor (CNS → muscle), relay (within CNS).

sperm.name: Sperm cell (male gamete)
sperm.rule: Carries the father's DNA to the egg.
examples: TAIL (flagellum): whips for swimming.
MANY MITOCHONDRIA in mid-piece: ATP energy for the swim.
ACROSOME at the head: enzymes to digest through the egg's outer layer.
HAPLOID nucleus: 23 chromosomes.
sperm.tip: Released in millions; only one fertilises the egg.

egg.name: Egg cell (ovum, female gamete)
egg.rule: Contains the mother's DNA + food and protection for the embryo.
examples: LARGE size: stores food (yolk).
JELLY COAT (zona pellucida): hardens after fertilisation to block other sperm.
HAPLOID nucleus: 23 chromosomes.
egg.tip: Released ~once a month at ovulation. Survives ~24 hours in the fallopian tube.

root.name: Root hair cell (plant)
root.rule: Absorbs water (by osmosis) and minerals (by active transport) from soil.
examples: LONG, THIN extension: massive surface area for absorption.
NO chloroplasts: roots don't photosynthesise.
MANY mitochondria: power active transport of minerals against the concentration gradient.
root.tip: Found in the 'zone of root hairs' just behind the growing root tip.

palisade.name: Palisade mesophyll cell (plant)
palisade.rule: Site of MOST photosynthesis in a leaf.
examples: PACKED with CHLOROPLASTS: maximises light absorption.
COLUMN shape: stacked tightly together to catch parallel light rays.
Located JUST BELOW the upper leaf surface: closest to incoming sunlight.
palisade.tip: Below palisade is the SPONGY MESOPHYLL — looser, with air spaces for gas exchange.

transport.title: Transport across membranes
transport.diffusion: Diffusion — movement from high to low concentration. Passive (no energy).
transport.osmosis: Osmosis — diffusion of WATER across a partially permeable membrane.
transport.activeTransport: Active transport — low to high concentration. Requires energy (ATP).

bodySystems.title: Body Systems
bodySystems.intro: How systems coordinate: circulation, respiration, digestion, nervous, hormonal.

circulatory.title: Circulatory system
circulatory.body: Double circulation: heart pumps blood through two loops (to lungs, to body). Heart has 4 chambers — left atrium, left ventricle, right atrium, right ventricle. Arteries carry blood from heart (thick walls, high pressure). Veins return blood to heart (thin walls, valves). Capillaries — site of exchange (one cell thick).

respiratory.title: Respiratory system
respiratory.body: Air path: nose → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli. Alveoli features for gas exchange: large surface area, thin walls (1 cell), rich blood supply, moist lining.

digestive.title: Digestive system
digestive.body: Mouth (mechanical + amylase) → oesophagus → stomach (HCl + pepsin) → small intestine (bile, pancreatic enzymes, absorption via villi) → large intestine (water absorption) → rectum → anus.

nervous.title: Nervous system
nervous.body: CNS = brain + spinal cord. PNS = sensory and motor neurones. Reflex arc: receptor → sensory neurone → relay neurone (spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector. Fast and involuntary.

endocrine.title: Endocrine (hormonal) system
endocrine.body: Hormones travel in blood, act on target organs. Slower but longer-lasting than nerves. Key hormones: insulin (pancreas, lowers blood sugar), adrenaline (adrenal glands, fight-or-flight), oestrogen/testosterone (puberty).

ecology.title: Ecology
ecology.intro: How organisms interact with each other and the environment. Energy flow, nutrient cycles, human impacts.

foodChains.title: Food chains and webs
foodChains.body: Producer (plant) → primary consumer (herbivore) → secondary consumer (carnivore) → tertiary consumer. Energy is lost at each step (~90%): only ~10% transferred. Pyramids of numbers, biomass, energy.

carbonCycle.title: Carbon cycle
carbonCycle.body: Photosynthesis fixes CO₂. Respiration releases it. Decomposers break down dead matter. Combustion of fossil fuels adds extra CO₂ (climate change link).

waterCycle.title: Water cycle
waterCycle.body: Evaporation → condensation (clouds) → precipitation → runoff/infiltration → back to oceans. Transpiration in plants is a key part.

humanImpact.title: Human impacts
humanImpact.body: Deforestation, pollution, habitat loss, climate change. Conservation: protected areas, sustainable farming, captive breeding.

dna.title: DNA
dna.body: Double helix. Bases A–T, C–G. A gene = section of DNA coding for a protein. Chromosomes contain DNA. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs).

mitosis.title: Mitosis
mitosis.body: Produces 2 identical diploid cells (2n). Used for growth and repair. Stages: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase.

meiosis.title: Meiosis
meiosis.body: Produces 4 genetically different haploid gametes (n). Used for sexual reproduction. Halves the chromosome number.

inheritance.title: Inheritance
inheritance.body: Dominant allele (capital) expressed if present. Recessive (lowercase) only when homozygous. Punnett square predicts offspring ratios.

variation.title: Variation
variation.body: Genetic (inherited) + environmental. Continuous (height, weight — many genes) vs discontinuous (blood type, eye colour — few genes).

evolution.title: Evolution by natural selection
evolution.body: 1) Variation exists in population. 2) Competition for resources. 3) Best-adapted survive and reproduce. 4) Advantageous alleles increase in frequency over generations.

immune.title: Immune System & Health
immune.intro: How the body defends itself. Pathogens, immune response, vaccination, antibiotics.

pathogens.title: Pathogens
pathogens.body: Disease-causing organisms: bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists. Spread by air, water, food, vectors (e.g., mosquitoes), direct contact.

barriers.title: Non-specific defences
barriers.body: Skin (physical barrier), mucus + cilia in airways, stomach acid, lysozyme in tears.

immuneResponse.title: Immune response (specific)
immuneResponse.body: White blood cells (lymphocytes) produce antibodies that bind to antigens on pathogens. Antibodies mark pathogens for destruction. Memory cells provide long-term immunity.

vaccination.title: Vaccination
vaccination.body: Inject weakened/dead pathogen → immune system makes antibodies + memory cells without illness. Next exposure → fast secondary response. Herd immunity protects vulnerable.

antibiotics.title: Antibiotics
antibiotics.body: Kill bacteria (not viruses). Overuse leads to resistance — must finish course. MRSA is an example of resistant bacteria.

experiment.title: Experimental Design (Criterion B)
experiment.intro: Designing a fair test: variables, hypothesis, method, controls.

items.controlled: Controlled — kept the same to ensure fair test

hypothesis.title: Hypothesis
hypothesis.body: Predicted relationship between IV and DV with reasoning. Format: 'If X increases, Y will decrease BECAUSE…'

method.title: Method
method.body: Step-by-step instructions someone else could follow. Include amounts, times, controlled variables, safety.

data.title: Data collection and analysis
data.body: Repeat for reliability. Calculate means. Plot suitable graphs (line for continuous, bar for discrete). Look for trends. Discuss anomalies.

evaluation.title: Evaluation
evaluation.body: Strengths and weaknesses of method. Sources of error (random vs systematic). Improvements. Extensions.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.intro: Key G9 Biology terms, EN with Russian equivalent.

immuneSystem.def: Body's defence network of cells, tissues and organs that protects against pathogens.

cellularRespiration.def: Process by which cells break down glucose with oxygen to release energy (ATP).

photosynthesis.def: Process by which plants convert sunlight, water and CO₂ into glucose and oxygen.

ecosystem.def: A community of living organisms together with the non-living components of their environment.

dna.def: Deoxyribonucleic acid — molecule carrying genetic information in cells.

evolution.def: Change in heritable characteristics of populations over generations through natural selection.

mitosis.def: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells. Used for growth and repair.

meiosis.def: Cell division producing four gametes with half the chromosome number.

foodWeb.def: Interlinked food chains showing energy flow through an ecosystem.

biodiversity.def: Variety of living organisms in a habitat — species, genetic and ecosystem diversity.
